01_initial_request.input_data: Distributional data, genetic data (SNPs, nuclear and plastid markers), and spatially explicit demographic models
01_initial_request.user_intent: To infer phylogeographic history and predict species response to future climate change by testing survival hypotheses
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Efficient statistical testing and validation of phylogeographic hypotheses

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Phylogeographic studies of alpine plants require distributional data to understand species ranges. [Gap] Raw occurrence data needs to be collected and prepared for modeling. [Decision] To achieve phylogeographic inference, chose to use distribution data collection to perform species occurrence recording on alpine plant populations, expecting to obtain presence/absence records for modeling.

parameters.target_species: Alpine plants (e.g., Carex nova, C. chalciolepis, Pedicularis asplenifolia, C. fuliginosa)
parameters.data_type: Distributional data, genetic data (SNPs, nuclear and plastid markers)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Distribution data and genetic data collected for alpine plant species

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Environmental layers are needed to construct ecological niche models. [Gap] Climate and environmental data must be obtained for relevant time periods. [Decision] To achieve ENM construction, chose to use environmental layer acquisition to perform climate data retrieval for present and Last Glacial Maximum periods, expecting to obtain bioclimatic variables for modeling.

parameters.time_periods: Present, Last Glacial Maximum (LGM, 22ka), Last Interglacial (LIG, 130-115ka)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Environmental layers obtained for present and past time periods

references.citation: Hijmans et al. 2005; Fick and Hijmans 2017
Karger et al. 2017; Karger et al. 2021

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Ecological niche models need to be constructed from distribution and environmental data. [Gap] Species habitat suitability must be modeled for different time periods. [Decision] To achieve habitat suitability modeling, chose to use MAXENT to perform ecological niche modeling on alpine plant distribution data, expecting to obtain ENMs for present and LGM periods.

parameters.assumption: No changes in ecological niche since past time period

02_agent_trajectory.observation: ENMs constructed for present and LGM time periods

references.relevance: MAXENT modeling tool for species geographic distributions
Applied MAXENT for alpine plant ENM construction

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Alternative phylogeographic hypotheses need to be tested. [Gap] ENMs must be modified to represent different survival scenarios. [Decision] To achieve hypothesis testing, chose to use BIOMOD to perform ecological niche modeling with ensemble forecasting on alpine plant data, expecting to obtain ENMs for model comparison.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: ENMs constructed using ensemble forecasting approach

references.citation: Thuiller et al. 2009; Thuiller et al. 2020
references.relevance: BIOMOD platform for ensemble species distribution modeling
Applied BIOMOD for European alpine plant ENM construction

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] ENMs need to be modified to test specific phylogeographic hypotheses. [Gap] Different survival scenarios must be represented in the models. [Decision] To achieve hypothesis testing, chose to use ENM modification to perform habitat suitability adjustment on grid cells, expecting to obtain modified ENMs representing different refugia scenarios.

parameters.modification_types: Setting habitat suitability to zero (barriers), reducing to 15% (permeable), upscaling resolution
parameters.threshold: 10% of maximum habitat suitability set to zero
parameters.scenarios: Peripheral survival only, peripheral + nunatak survival, nunatak survival only
parameters.ice_sheet_reduction: 15% of original habitat suitability in ice-covered regions

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Modified ENMs created for different phylogeographic hypotheses (barrier vs permeable, peripheral vs nunatak)

references.relevance: Modified ENMs for testing nunatak survival hypotheses
Modified ENMs for testing microhabitat preference hypotheses

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Modified ENMs need to be converted to input format for demographic simulation. [Gap] ENM data must be translated into SPLATCHE-compatible format. [Decision] To achieve demographic simulation input preparation, chose to use ENM conversion script to perform habitat suitability translation on grid cells, expecting to obtain SPLATCHE input files.

parameters.origin: Script originally devised by He et al. 2013
parameters.time_period_limit: Can only handle two time periods
parameters.conversion_method: Translating changes in habitat suitability between different time periods into classes of change

02_agent_trajectory.observation: SPLATCHE input files generated from modified ENMs

references.relevance: Original script for converting ENMs to SPLATCHE input

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Demographic history needs to be simulated across the landscape. [Gap] Population dynamics must be modeled forward in time. [Decision] To achieve demographic history simulation, chose to use SPLATCHE 3 to perform forward-time demographic simulation on grid cell demes, expecting to obtain demographic history over studied time period.

parameters.simulation_direction: Forward in time from past to present
parameters.grid_cells: Each treated as a deme (panmictic group)
parameters.carrying_capacity: Defined by habitat suitability from ENM
parameters.features: Long distance dispersal, spatially explicit evolutionary scenarios

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Demographic history obtained including population sizes at sampled demes

references.relevance: SPLATCHE3 simulation of serial genetic data under spatially explicit scenarios
Applied SPLATCHE 3 for alpine plant demographic simulations

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Genetic data needs to be simulated based on demographic history. [Gap] Genealogies must be generated using coalescent theory. [Decision] To achieve genetic data simulation, chose to use SPLATCHE 3 to perform backward-time coalescent simulation on demographic history, expecting to obtain simulated genealogies and genetic data.

parameters.simulation_direction: Backwards in time using coalescent theory

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Simulated genealogies and genetic data generated

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Summary statistics need to be calculated from simulated genetic data. [Gap] Genetic patterns must be quantified for model comparison. [Decision] To achieve statistical comparison, chose to use ARLSUMSTAT to perform summary statistics calculation on simulated genetic data, expecting to obtain summary statistics for ABC analysis.

parameters.statistics_types: Number of segregating sites, mean heterozygosity, pairwise population FST
parameters.scope: For each population and across populations, across loci

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Summary statistics calculated from simulated genetic data

references.relevance: ARLSUMSTAT program for population genetics analyses

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Model selection requires statistical framework for comparing hypotheses. [Gap] Posterior distributions need to be approximated for parameter estimation. [Decision] To achieve model selection, chose to use ABCTOOLBOX to perform Approximate Bayesian Computation on summary statistics, expecting to obtain marginal densities and Bayes factors for model comparison.

parameters.sampling: Rejection-sampling with regression adjustment and weighting
parameters.prior_distributions: Parameter values drawn from priors (migration rates, ancestral population sizes)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Marginal densities calculated, Bayes factors computed for model selection

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Model fit needs to be assessed beyond marginal density. [Gap] Goodness-of-fit must be evaluated to ensure model adequacy. [Decision] To achieve model validation, chose to use goodness-of-fit assessment to perform p-value calculation on best fitting runs, expecting to obtain p-values representing proportion of runs with likelihood higher or equal to observed data.

parameters.interpretation: Proportion of best fitting runs with likelihood higher or equal to observed data
parameters.threshold_concern: Low p-value indicates best model is among poor models

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) with Bayes factors and goodness-of-fit p-values

bayes_factor_threshold_significant.interpretation: Bayes factor greater than 3.2 considered significant

bayes_factor_threshold_strong.interpretation: Bayes factor between 10 and 100 considered strong support

bayes_factor_threshold_decisive.interpretation: Bayes factor above 100 considered decisive support

carex_nova_barrier_model_bayes_factor.interpretation: Strong support for barrier model in wet-adapted C. nova

carex_chalciolepis_permeable_model_bayes.interpretation: Weak support for permeable model in dry-adapted C. chalciolepis

pedicularis_peri_east_nun_bayes_factor.interpretation: Decisive support for eastern periphery plus nunatak survival model

carex_fuliginosa_peri_east_bayes_factor.interpretation: Decisive support for eastern periphery survival model

goodness_of_fit_p_value_range.interpretation: High p-values indicate good reconstruction of observed data

simulation_count_per_model.interpretation: One million simulations generated per model for adequate sampling

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The iDDC approach successfully achieved the research objective of efficient statistical testing and validation of phylogeographic hypotheses for alpine plants. Case studies demonstrated species-specific responses to Pleistocene climate change, with strong support for nunatak survival in cold-adapted species (Bayes factor >100) and microhabitat-dependent survival strategies (barrier vs permeable models). The method effectively integrated distributional data, spatially explicit demographic modeling, and coalescent simulations to test long-standing hypotheses about alpine plant survival during the Last Glacial Maximum.
